summary: This lesson introduces the Spanish future tense, showing that it is formed by adding haber-derived endings to the full infinitive. The mnemonic of 'pushing the accent into the future' is taught. The lesson also reviews the three ways to express future: present tense with context, ir a + infinitive, and the future tense.

teachings.summary: The future tense is formed by adding endings derived from haber to the full infinitive. The endings are: -é (I), -ás (you informal), -á (he/she/it/you formal), -emos (we), -án (they/you guys). The accent always falls on the ending, 'pushing the accent into the future'. Works the same for -ar, -er, and -ir verbs.
teachings.rule: Take the full infinitive + add haber-derived endings: -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -án. The accent is always on the ending. Think out the whole verb first before adding the ending (preparar + é = prepararé, not preparé).
teachings.notes: Common mistake: saying 'preparé' instead of 'prepararé' by not thinking out the whole verb first. The future tense endings match haber forms (he/hemos/ha/has/han). Ir is regular in the future tense despite being very irregular in the present.

teachings.summary: Quick recap of haber forms using the two-vowel trick: /e/ gives he (I have) and hemos (we have); /a/ gives ha (he/she has), has (you informal have), han (they have).
teachings.notes: With haber structures, the object pronoun (lo, la, etc.) must come before haber. There is no option of placement.

teachings.summary: Reviewed as one of the ways to express future. You can use present tense with context, ir a + infinitive, or the future tense. The future tense is not used as often as the other two methods.

examples.english: I'm having breakfast with them tomorrow. (present)
I'm going to have breakfast with them. (ir a)

teachings.notes: Having future context doesn't prevent using ir a. Both structures can coexist with mañana.
